meta.title: Evolution AI Platform Tutor Level Framework
meta.subject: GCSE Citizenship Studies (England, grades 1-9)
meta.notClaimed: This framework supports platform design and teaching placement. It is not an official exam grade, certification, DfE endorsement, or Ofsted approval. Grade boundaries are set by exam boards each year.

aoDefinitions.ao1: Demonstrate knowledge and understanding of citizenship concepts, terms, and issues (30%)
aoDefinitions.ao2: Apply knowledge and understanding of citizenship concepts, terms, and issues to contexts and actions (30%)
aoDefinitions.ao3: Analyse and evaluate evidence relating to citizenship issues, debates, and actions, including different viewpoints, to develop reasoned arguments and substantiated judgements (40%)

common.note: AOs and weightings are set by Ofqual and identical across AQA, Edexcel, and OCR GCSE Citizenship specifications.

sections.title: Active citizenship
sections.content: Others' citizenship actions plus student's own investigation (research, action, reflection)

sections.title: Life in modern Britain
sections.content: British values, media, UK global role

sections.title: Democracy at work in the UK
Citizenship issues and debates
sections.note: Approx 21% of qualification; includes 12-mark and 15-mark extended parts

edexcel1CS0.note: Edexcel uses levels-based mark schemes with examiner commentary on development beyond simple statements for explain questions.

ocrJ270.note: OCR wording for AOs aligns with Ofqual; Paper 2 focuses on citizenship action planning, taking, and evaluation.

markSchemePatterns.pointMarking: Award 1 mark for identifying a valid point; award a second mark for development, explanation, or example linked to context.
markSchemePatterns.defineExample: Award 1 mark for correct definition and 1 mark for developed definition or appropriate example.

typical8Mark.level4: 7-8: Developed and sustained analysis; wide range of evidence and views; reasoned justifications; coherent argument
typical8Mark.level3: 5-6: Analysis of range of evidence; arguments not always fully developed or evaluated
typical8Mark.level2: 3-4: Basic analysis; limited range; weak arguments; limited viewpoints
typical8Mark.level1: 1-2: Limited evidence; insufficient viewpoints; little analysis or integration

typical12MarkActiveCitizenship.level4: 10-12: Clear precise analysis with well chosen investigation evidence; developed justified evaluated within citizenship action context
typical12MarkActiveCitizenship.level3: 7-9: Appropriate evidence; good application; analyses not always fully developed
typical12MarkActiveCitizenship.level2: 4-6: Some evidence; weak arguments; analysis not always relevant to context
typical12MarkActiveCitizenship.level1: 1-3: Basic application; little analysis or integration

examinerPrinciples: Credit other valid points not listed in indicative content
Use best-fit approach when answer spans levels
Explain requires more than a simple statement to access full marks (Edexcel examiner report 2023)
Active citizenship answers must link aims, research, action, and reflection

aoExpectations.ao1: Spot or recall isolated citizenship terms with heavy scaffolding; no full definitions expected
aoExpectations.ao2: Not expected independently; may select correct option in supported scenario

levels.aoFocus: AO1 entry recall with heavy scaffolding
studentCapabilities: Recognise familiar citizenship words when supported
Match terms to labels or sentence starters
Complete one-step tasks with prompting
Engage with very short activities
mustDemonstrate: Recognise at least 3 to 5 high-frequency citizenship terms when given options (vote, law, right, community, government)
Select a correct label from a word bank or picture match in one attempt with support
Respond to a single-step prompt without multi-part stacking
Attempt engagement even when answer is incomplete

markSchemeBehaviours.oneMark: Correct selection or naming from provided options; no development required
markSchemeBehaviours.twoMark: Not targeted; if attempted, credit only the identification half of a point-mark pair
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Random guessing without reading; copying question stem; no response
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Nothing to credit if off-topic; award mark for each correct identification up to maximum

exampleQuestionStems: Name one word that describes how people choose their MPs.
Identify which of these is a right: A) free speech B) paying a fine C) driving only
Give one example of a rule in school.
State what a vote is used for.
scaffoldingRules: One idea per chat message; never stack two questions
Always offer word bank, visuals, or binary choice
Read-aloud friendly phrasing; max 8 words per sentence in prompts
Celebrate any attempt before correcting
Glossary pinned and always visible
If wrong, rephrase once with fewer options; do not penalise

worksheetStyle.exitTask: Circle the correct word from three options about rights or rules in school
worksheetStyle.markingHint: 1 mark per correct identification; no written sentence required

criteria: 80%+ accuracy on identification tasks with word bank over 3 sessions
Can name one term without options in at least 2 topic areas
Attempts a full sentence when given a stem (e.g. 'A law is...')
blockers: Relies on guessing when options removed

citizenshipTopics.rights: Match 'right' to everyday examples (speak in class, fair treatment)
citizenshipTopics.responsibilities: Identify school rules as responsibilities; follow one-step scenarios
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Label voting, election, MP from picture prompts
citizenshipTopics.law: Sort 'legal' vs 'not allowed in school' with support
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Recognise words campaign, petition, volunteer from images only

teacherPractice: Pre-teach 3 to 5 terms per lesson with visuals before any written task
Use structured talk (think-pair-share) before individual response
ROG or equivalent colour bands: red tasks = identify/name only
TA or peer reader for all text-heavy prompts
Oracy first: student says answer, then selects from options

delivery.chatbotStyle: one idea per message; glossaries always visible

goodVsNext.good: Selects correct label from given options
goodVsNext.nextLevel: Recalls term without word bank and describes one feature in a full sentence

aoExpectations.ao1: Demonstrate knowledge of some aspects of concepts, terms, or issues with guided examples
aoExpectations.ao2: Minimal; may restate an example when given

levels.aoFocus: AO1 core terms with guided examples
studentCapabilities: Recall core citizenship terms in familiar topics
Complete simple definitions with models
Follow two-step instructions
Show awareness that citizenship involves rules and participation
mustDemonstrate: Define a core term with key word included when a model is shown first
Name one valid example from a familiar topic (one mark style)
Describe one characteristic of an institution (school council, police, Parliament) in 1 to 2 sentences
Follow read-then-answer two-step instructions

markSchemeBehaviours.oneMark: Correct identification or single valid point from recall
markSchemeBehaviours.twoMark: 1 mark correct point + 1 mark simple example or keyword in definition (AQA pattern: definition + developed example)
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Vague definitions ('democracy is fair'); lists without selecting one answer
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Award one mark for correct function/point; award second mark for appropriate example

exampleQuestionStems: Define the term 'democracy'.
Name one responsibility of a citizen in the UK.
Describe one feature of the UK Parliament.
Identify two ways people can take part in society.
scaffoldingRules: Show model answer before 'your turn' variant
Gap-fill definitions with keyword bank
Limit to one new term per activity block
MC with 3 to 4 options before open response
Feedback names the mark earned ('that is your identification mark')

worksheetStyle.format: Gap fill, name-one, define-with-word-bank, 2-mark describe one feature
worksheetStyle.exitTask: Define 'right' and give one school example (2 marks)
worksheetStyle.markingHint: 1+1 point marking; second mark needs linked example

criteria: Secure 1+1 marks on define questions in 2+ topics without model visible
Describes one feature with own words in full sentence
Beginning to explain 'why' with one because-clause when prompted

citizenshipTopics.rights: Define human right; name one HRA example; describe one school right
citizenshipTopics.responsibilities: Name responsibilities at home/school/nation; define rule of law simply
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Define democracy; describe general election in one step; name one way to participate
citizenshipTopics.law: Define law; identify magistrates court vs Crown Court at label level
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Define campaign; name one type of action (petition, fundraiser)

teacherPractice: I do, we do, you do for every define task
Vocabulary lists on desk; remove gradually across unit
Foundation tier exit tickets: name/define only, no extended writing
Pair weaker writers with structured note-taker
Retrieval drills on 10 core terms weekly

delivery.chatbotStyle: model answer first, then your turn variant

goodVsNext.good: Defines a term including the key word
goodVsNext.nextLevel: Explains why the term matters in a familiar context without a model

aoExpectations.ao1: Knowledge of some aspects across at least one theme; near grade 2 Ofqual anchor
aoExpectations.ao2: Early application in familiar local or school contexts
aoExpectations.ao3: May state an opinion with tutor-prompted because; limited evidence

levels.aoFocus: AO1 plus early AO2 in familiar contexts
studentCapabilities: Describe features of familiar institutions
Use subject terms with growing accuracy
Attempt short explanations with sentence starters
Show some awareness of differing viewpoints
mustDemonstrate: Describe 2 linked features of a familiar institution (grade 2: some aspects knowledge)
Explain one reason using 'because' in a school or local context
Apply a concept to a given scenario with sentence frame (e.g. rights in school)
State one other viewpoint when prompted ('Some people think... however...')

markSchemeBehaviours.oneMark: Single valid feature or reason identified
markSchemeBehaviours.twoMark: Describe/explain with one developed link (reason connected to context)
markSchemeBehaviours.fourMarkPlus: Emerging only; 3 to 4 mark explain may score 1 to 2 without development
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Opinion without because; describes without citizenship vocabulary
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Basic line of reasoning with some awareness of differing viewpoints; limited reference to evidence

exampleQuestionStems: Describe how a school council gives students a voice.
Explain why voting matters in a local election.
Apply the idea of responsibility to this scenario: litter in the park.
Outline two features of the UK police service.
scaffoldingRules: Paragraph frames with Because / This means / For example labels
Optional stretch: second reason only after first is praised
Scenarios set in school/local before national
Viewpoint sentence starters provided but not filled in
Praise partial answers; one follow-up question maximum

worksheetStyle.format: 2-mark short answer; describe + explain two-part; simple scenario box
worksheetStyle.exitTask: Explain one reason why young people might join a youth club (2 marks)

criteria: Consistent 2/2 on familiar-topic explain questions
Applies concept to new but similar scenario with 2 linked reasons
Uses at least one subject term accurately per answer
blockers: Needs sentence frame for every answer

citizenshipTopics.rights: Explain why free speech has limits in school; describe student rights
citizenshipTopics.responsibilities: Apply responsibilities to scenario (cyberbullying, voting age debate at simple level)
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Describe local council role; explain why people vote
citizenshipTopics.law: Outline youth justice approach; explain one purpose of law
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Describe one stage of investigation (research or action); explain why consultation helps

teacherPractice: Grade 2 descriptor as success criteria displayed on board
Structured talk to rehearse 'because' clauses before writing
Orange/ROG band: describe + one explain
Mix closed then open questions within same lesson
Use familiar contexts exclusively before national examples

delivery.chatbotStyle: praise partial answers; optional stretch explain why

goodVsNext.nextLevel: Applies concept to a new scenario with two linked reasons

aoExpectations.ao1: Adequate knowledge of core content; some inaccuracies acceptable
aoExpectations.ao2: Apply knowledge to local and national contexts with structured practice
aoExpectations.ao3: Basic consideration of an issue without balanced evaluation

levels.aoFocus: AO1 and AO2 with structured practice
studentCapabilities: Explain causes and effects in straightforward topics
Apply rights and responsibilities to short scenarios
Structure templated answers with beginning, middle, end
Use evidence from a single source when directed
mustDemonstrate: Explain with at least one developed reason linked to context (3 to 4 mark questions)
Apply rights/responsibilities to a short unseen scenario
Use PEE/PEEL-style structure when template provided
Reference one line from a source when told which line to use

markSchemeBehaviours.oneMark: Valid point identified but not developed
markSchemeBehaviours.twoMark: Point + development/explanation (Edexcel: explain needs more than simple statement)
markSchemeBehaviours.fourMark: Two developed points OR one well-developed point + application to context
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Two sides listed with no link to question; undeveloped explain ('people should vote because it is important')
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Award 1 mark for identifying; 1 mark for developing explanation as to why

exampleQuestionStems: Explain two ways Parliament holds the government to account.
Apply the concept of human rights to a scenario about stop and search.
Consider whether this source supports the view that media is fair.
Outline the main features of the UK justice system.
scaffoldingRules: Introduce PEE with citizenship labels (Point, Evidence, Link to citizenship concept)
Break scenarios into bullet steps; student answers step 1 before step 2
One follow-up after first answer, not parallel questions
Source: highlight line numbers before explain task
Remove sentence stems gradually within unit

worksheetStyle.format: 3 to 4 mark explain; scenario apply; source highlight + explain
worksheetStyle.exitTask: Explain two ways a citizen can influence local planning (4 marks)
worksheetStyle.markingHint: 2 x (identify + develop) or equivalent point marking

criteria: Regular 3+/4 on explain without paragraph frame
Applies to national context with accurate terms
Uses source evidence unprompted once per answer
blockers: Explain answers remain one mark only

citizenshipTopics.rights: Apply HRA to scenario; explain balanced rights vs security
citizenshipTopics.responsibilities: Explain jury service; apply consumer responsibilities
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Explain role of MPs vs Lords; consider voter turnout issue
citizenshipTopics.law: Outline civil vs criminal; explain one youth sentencing aim
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Explain how primary research assisted investigation; consider one difficulty faced

teacherPractice: Master foundational knowledge before source analysis (ACT guidance)
3/4 grade band tasks with writing frames available not mandatory
Modelling explain with 'this would be 1 mark... this development earns the 2nd'
Chunk sources: read, highlight, then answer
Formative marking using point + development language

delivery.chatbotStyle: break scenarios into steps; one follow-up after first answer

goodVsNext.nextLevel: Applies to unfamiliar context with accurate terminology throughout

aoExpectations.ao1: Mostly accurate knowledge of a range of relevant aspects; grade 5 trajectory
aoExpectations.ao2: Secure application to contexts and citizenship actions
aoExpectations.ao3: Reasoned arguments with some differing viewpoints; evidence-based conclusions forming

studentCapabilities: Mostly accurate knowledge across multiple themes
Apply concepts to national and some global contexts
Construct reasoned points with some evidence
Compare two simple viewpoints on familiar issues
mustDemonstrate: Mostly accurate knowledge across 2+ themes in one response (grade 5 trajectory)
Reasoned paragraph with one viewpoint and supporting evidence
Compare or discuss with brief counter-view
Link citizenship action example to outcome (basic evaluation)

markSchemeBehaviours.fourMark: Two reasoned points with some evidence OR one point with viewpoint comparison
markSchemeBehaviours.sixMark: Level 2 to 3 on 6-mark source: some evidence selected; arguments not always fully developed
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Knowledge list without application; discuss without conclusion
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Reasoned arguments considering some differing viewpoints; evidence-based conclusions

exampleQuestionStems: Discuss one benefit and one drawback of lowering the voting age to 16.
Compare how two sources present views on immigration.
Apply knowledge of pressure groups to explain how Greenpeace influences policy.
Explain whether your citizenship issue was mainly local, national, or global.
scaffoldingRules: Structure suggested (intro / point / counter / conclusion) not pre-filled
Light mark-scheme feedback: 'developed point', 'needs evidence'
Discuss tasks: require both sides before marking complete
Encourage precise terms over vague language ('legitimacy' not 'fairness')
Planning box optional; 1 paragraph minimum expectation

worksheetStyle.format: 4 to 6 mark explain/apply; source short answer; discuss with mark hint
worksheetStyle.exitTask: Discuss whether social media helps political participation (6 marks)
worksheetStyle.markingHint: Point marking for short items; levels emerging for 6-mark discuss

criteria: Grade 5 descriptor elements in 2+ marked tasks (range of knowledge, some viewpoints)
Independently weighs two viewpoints in scenario
6-mark responses reach level 2 to 3 consistently
blockers: Counter-argument only present when template given
Evidence generic not linked to source

citizenshipTopics.rights: Discuss privacy vs security; compare HRA and UDHR application
citizenshipTopics.responsibilities: Apply legal aid debate; discuss responsibilities of media
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Discuss voting systems; compare participation methods
citizenshipTopics.law: Compare roles of courts; discuss stop and search balancing rights
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Discuss most successful investigation stage with evidence; evaluate usefulness of research

teacherPractice: Grade 5 anchor descriptors as rubric headers
Green/ROG band: discuss with both sides
Modelling counter-argument explicitly
Link Paper 1 active citizenship vocabulary to Paper 2 themes
Peer assess using 'identify + develop' checklist

goodVsNext.good: Reasoned paragraph with one viewpoint and evidence
goodVsNext.nextLevel: Independently weighs two viewpoints in a scenario response

aoExpectations.ao1: Accurate knowledge integrated into answers; grade 5 Ofqual anchor secure
aoExpectations.ao2: Confident application across local, national, and global contexts
aoExpectations.ao3: Evidence-based analysis; evaluate participation outcomes; some differing viewpoints

levels.aoFocus: AO2 and AO3 in scenario responses
studentCapabilities: Construct reasoned arguments considering some differing viewpoints
Apply knowledge fluently to scenario prompts
Analyse simple sources for bias or purpose
Link participation actions to outcomes with evidence
mustDemonstrate: Balanced discuss with conclusion (grade 5: reasoned arguments, some viewpoints)
Analyse source for purpose/bias with evidence quote
Evaluate citizenship action outcome with one criterion
Integrate knowledge into argument rather than listing facts

markSchemeBehaviours.oneMark: Valid identification in complex question (building block only)
markSchemeBehaviours.fourMark: Not typical standalone; subsumed in levels marking
markSchemeBehaviours.sixToEightMark: Level 3 (5-6): analysis of range of evidence; arguments not always fully evaluated; conclusion may lack coherence
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Evaluate without criteria; analyse describes rather than separates components
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Evaluate outcomes of participation; credible research enquiries; evidence-based analysis

exampleQuestionStems: Analyse how Source A presents a biased view of young voters.
Evaluate how successful a local litter campaign was in meeting its aims.
Discuss the strengths and weaknesses of the first-past-the-post system.
Examine why a pressure group campaign might fail to achieve its aims.
scaffoldingRules: Planning box only; no paragraph frames
Challenge vague claims: 'what evidence supports that?'
Require named evaluative criteria before judging success
Source analysis: must quote or paraphrase specific detail
Multi-paragraph allowed; coach linking sentences

worksheetStyle.format: 6 to 8 mark scenario; source analysis; discuss both sides; extended paragraph
worksheetStyle.exitTask: Evaluate the success of a school citizenship action using evidence (8 marks)
worksheetStyle.markingHint: Levels 2 to 3 target; credit analysis of evidence and participation outcomes

criteria: Balanced 8-mark responses at level 3+ in 3 consecutive tasks
Maps own answer to AO1/AO2/AO3 when asked
Evaluates with strengths and limitations explicitly
blockers: Source analysis is summary not analysis

citizenshipTopics.rights: Evaluate HRA reform proposals; analyse media source on privacy
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Analyse low turnout causes; evaluate e-petitions effectiveness
citizenshipTopics.law: Evaluate youth sentencing aims; analyse source on legal aid cuts
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Evaluate investigation methodology; analyse own action against citizenship action definition

teacherPractice: Secure grade 5 before pushing grade 7 techniques
Use real mark schemes for 6 and 8 mark questions in lessons
Remove writing frames; keep planning templates
Timed sections building to half-exam pace
Explicit teaching of bias, purpose, reliability in sources

delivery.chatbotStyle: challenge vague claims; ask for evidence

goodVsNext.nextLevel: Explicit mark-scheme targeting across AO1, AO2, AO3

aoExpectations.ao1: Accurate, relevant knowledge selected for question focus
aoExpectations.ao2: Strong application including citizenship actions and varied contexts
aoExpectations.ao3: Reasoned coherent arguments; multiple perspectives; substantiated judgements

levels.aoFocus: AO2 and AO3 with mark-scheme thinking
studentCapabilities: Exam-style timing and response length discipline
Map answers to AO mark allocations
Analyse sources selectively for relevance
Evaluate with criteria and evidence-based conclusions
mustDemonstrate: Hit level 2 to 3 descriptors consistently on 8 to 12 mark questions
Select relevant knowledge for question focus (not everything known)
Justify judgement with substantiated evidence from source and own knowledge
Complete active citizenship exam-style linkage: aims, research, action, reflection

markSchemeBehaviours.twoMark: Mini developed point inside 8+ mark answer
markSchemeBehaviours.eightMark: Level 3 (5-6): range of evidence; arguments not always fully developed; conclusion may wobble
markSchemeBehaviours.twelveMark: Level 3 (7-9) on active citizenship: good application; analyses not always fully evaluated
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Knowledge dump; running out of time; no substantiated final judgement
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Best fit level then mark within level; coherent arguments; substantiated judgements

exampleQuestionStems: Assess the importance of the rule of law in a democratic society.
Analyse the ways your investigation was a good example of taking citizenship action.
To what extent do you agree that the UK media supports democracy?
Justify your choice of action in your active citizenship investigation.
scaffoldingRules: Exam conditions optional mode; timed prompts
Coaching question: 'What would gain the next mark?'
Require AO labelling in review only, not in live exam practice
No hints after first planning message in timed mode
Feedback references level descriptors verbatim

worksheetStyle.format: 8 to 12 mark extended; multi-source; active citizenship evaluation
worksheetStyle.exitTask: 12-mark: Analyse how your investigation linked research to action (timed 15 min)
worksheetStyle.markingHint: Best fit level; active citizenship needs AO2 linkage + AO3 justification

criteria: Level 3+ on 8-mark and 12-mark tasks in 4+ papers
Exam timing: finishes with 2 min review spare
Self-identifies missing developed point in mark scheme review

citizenshipTopics.rights: Assess trade-offs in human rights enforcement; multi-source rights debate
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Assess representation of young people in Parliament
citizenshipTopics.law: Evaluate effectiveness of sentencing aims; assess legal aid access
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Full Paper 1 Section A style: summarise research, evaluate usefulness, analyse citizenship action quality

teacherPractice: Mock marking with level descriptors on desk
Interleave exam questions across themes (retrieval + application)
Dedicated active citizenship exam technique sessions
Peer marking with mark scheme training (ACT/exam board CPD model)
Fix timing before pushing content breadth

delivery.chatbotStyle: what would gain the next mark coaching; timed practice

goodVsNext.nextLevel: Perceptive analysis across themes with minimal prompting

aoExpectations.ao1: Comprehensive relevant knowledge woven through argument; grade 8 trajectory
aoExpectations.ao2: Sophisticated application including local and global comparison of actions
aoExpectations.ao3: Well-substantiated conclusions; critical evaluation; coherent evidence synthesis

studentCapabilities: Comprehensive knowledge across wide range of concepts, terms, issues
Perceptive arguments considering variety of viewpoints
Ambitious enquiry framing with strong evidence-based analysis
Critical evaluation of citizenship action effectiveness
mustDemonstrate: Level 4 descriptors on 8-mark questions (7-8: developed sustained analysis, coherent argument)
Variety of viewpoints considered (grade 8 anchor)
Critical evaluation of citizenship action outcomes
Well-substantiated conclusion that follows from evidence

markSchemeBehaviours.eightMark: Level 4 (7-8): developed sustained analysis; wide range of evidence and views; reasoned justifications; coherent argument
markSchemeBehaviours.twelveMark: Level 4 (10-12): clear precise analysis; well chosen investigation evidence; leads to well-judged coherent conclusion
markSchemeBehaviours.sixteenMark: Level 3 to 4: perceptive arguments; may approach sustained synthesis
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Strong opening but drift; evaluation descriptive; viewpoints tokenistic
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Perceptive convincing arguments; well-substantiated conclusions; critical evaluation of participation outcomes

exampleQuestionStems: Evaluate the extent to which UK citizens can influence government decisions.
Debate whether economic sanctions are an effective tool of global citizenship.
Conclude whether your citizenship action achieved its aims, using evidence from all investigation stages.
Analyse how two campaigns used different strategies to achieve change.
scaffoldingRules: Minimal scaffolding; student-led essay plan
Socratic follow-ups only: probe weak links
Require explicit conclusion matching command word
Cross-theme links rewarded in feedback
No sentence starters; optional planning checklist only

worksheetStyle.format: 12 to 16 mark evaluation; cross-theme synoptic; compare actions/debates
worksheetStyle.exitTask: 16-mark: Evaluate the extent to which protest strengthens democracy (timed 20 min)
worksheetStyle.markingHint: Level 4 requires sustained analysis + coherent substantiated conclusion

criteria: Level 4 on 8 and 12 mark questions regularly
Grade 8 descriptor met in summative mock
Cross-theme synthesis in at least one response per unit

citizenshipTopics.rights: Synoptic: link HRA, media freedom, and participation rights in one argument
citizenshipTopics.responsibilities: Evaluate global citizenship responsibilities on climate
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Debate constitutional reform; evaluate deliberative democracy
citizenshipTopics.law: Critical evaluation of justice system reform proposals
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Critically evaluate own action impact with counterfactual (what if different action)

teacherPractice: Grade 8 descriptor as stretch target for all extended writing
Synoptic starters linking 2 themes
Student-led seminars with counter-argument role cards
Compare exemplar level 3 vs level 4 scripts
Ambitious enquiry questions for homework reading

goodVsNext.nextLevel: Exceptional cross-theme synthesis and examiner-level precision

aoExpectations.ao1: Wide-ranging precise knowledge deployed selectively; grade 8-9 standard
aoExpectations.ao2: Transfer application to novel, complex, or cross-context scenarios
aoExpectations.ao3: Perceptive convincing arguments; multiple viewpoints; highest-band substantiated judgements

studentCapabilities: Exceptional comprehensive knowledge across themes
Perceptive convincing arguments with examiner-ready structure
Synthesise themes in unified arguments
Critically evaluate participation and policy with substantiated judgements
mustDemonstrate: Grade 8 descriptor secure plus grade 9 sophistication (insight, precision, no drift)
Synthesise 2+ themes in unified argument (e.g. rights + media + participation)
Perceptive evaluation with nuanced trade-offs acknowledged
Examiner-ready QWC: specialist vocabulary, coherent structure, substantiated judgement

markSchemeBehaviours.eightMark: Top of level 4: as good as can be expected; sustained line without drift
markSchemeBehaviours.twelveMark: Full marks band: analysis developed, justified, evaluated within citizenship action context throughout
markSchemeBehaviours.sixteenPlusMark: Synoptic: selective comprehensive knowledge; perceptive cross-viewpoint synthesis
markSchemeBehaviours.typicalErrors: Over-writing without focus; sophistication without evidence; novelty without substantiation
markSchemeBehaviours.examinerLanguage: Perceptive and convincing; ambitious enquiry; critical evaluation; highest-band substantiated judgements

exampleQuestionStems: Evaluate the extent to which digital citizenship has transformed political participation in the UK.
Argue whether the UK should adopt a written constitution, considering rights, democracy, and law.
Assess how effectively citizenship action can address structural inequality.
Conclude, with substantiated judgement, whether national interest ever justifies limiting human rights.
scaffoldingRules: No scaffolding by default; teacher override only
Extension: counter-argument and meta-reflection on argument quality
Peer/examiner register in prompts
Challenge to tighten selective knowledge deployment
Optional timed full paper sections

worksheetStyle.format: 16+ mark synoptic; evaluate extent; cross-unit debate; citizenship action critique
worksheetStyle.exitTask: Synoptic 20-mark style: rights, democracy, and active citizenship in one evaluative essay
worksheetStyle.markingHint: Grade 9: insight and precision beyond secure grade 8; top of level 4 on all extended items
worksheetStyle.timeGuidance: 22 to 25 minutes; full paper practice in revision phase

criteria: This is the top band; readiness = maintain grade 8-9 under full exam conditions
Consistent top-of-level-4 across papers
Teacher confirms no further band increase needed

citizenshipTopics.rights: Grade 9 synoptic: HRA, international law, digital rights, protest rights unified
citizenshipTopics.responsibilities: Evaluate competing responsibilities: citizen, consumer, global actor
citizenshipTopics.democracy: Perceptive analysis of democratic deficit and reform pathways
citizenshipTopics.law: Evaluate interplay of common law, statute, and international treaties
citizenshipTopics.activeCitizenship: Critique own and others' actions with methodological and ethical evaluation

teacherPractice: Extension reading from reputable news and Hansard
Student as examiner: mark unknown scripts
Full papers under timed conditions monthly in Y11
Cross-board question comparison (AQA/Edexcel/OCR style)
Push selective knowledge not exhaustive listing

goodVsNext.nextLevel: Grade 9 exceptional insight, precision, and sustained argument
